stories.thread: The End of the 1960s and Mad Men
stories.story: The end arrived for the 1960s with significant events, including the Rolling Stones' Altamont fiasco in December 1969, the Kent State shootings in May 1970, or Richard Nixon's 1972 re-election. For the show "Mad Men," the "end of an era," as its slogan has it, begins Sunday. Over the past eight years, the show about a 1960s advertising agency and its collision with changing times has become part of the national fabric, if never a huge ratings hit.
key_facts: 1960s end events: Rolling Stones' Altamont fiasco (December 1969), Kent State shootings (May 1970), Richard Nixon's 1972 re-election
"Mad Men" show's slogan: "end of an era"
"Mad Men" begins Sunday
"Mad Men" has run for eight years
"Mad Men" is about a 1960s advertising agency
"Mad Men" is part of the national fabric
"Mad Men" was never a huge ratings hit

stories.thread: The End of the 1960s and Mad Men
stories.story: The show "Mad Men," which has run for the past eight years, has deeply impacted the national fabric. Stores have created fashion lines inspired by the show, and there have been "Mad Men" cocktails and museum exhibits. Even "Mad Men" presidential references have emerged, showing its cultural reach. Don Draper, the creative director played by Jon Hamm, has become a symbol of the times, both his and, sometimes, ours. In March, a "Mad Men" bench was unveiled in front of New York's Time & Life Building, where the fictional firm of Sterling Cooper & Partners has its headquarters.
key_facts: "Mad Men" show's cultural impact: fashion lines, cocktails, museum exhibits, presidential references
Don Draper, the creative director played by Jon Hamm, is a symbol of the times
A "Mad Men" bench was unveiled in March
The bench is in front of New York's Time & Life Building
Sterling Cooper & Partners is a fictional firm with headquarters in New York's Time & Life Building

stories.story: The end of a TV series brings with it some risk, as seen with "The Sopranos." "The Sopranos," which was "Mad Men" creator Matthew Weiner's former employer, divided fans with its famous cut-to-black finale. On the other hand, "Mad Men's" former AMC stablemate, "Breaking Bad," was saluted for an almost perfect landing. The last season of "Mad Men," technically the first half of season 7, ended with the moon landing in July 1969.
key_facts: TV series finales carry risk
"The Sopranos" divided fans with its cut-to-black finale
"The Sopranos" was Matthew Weiner's former employer
"Breaking Bad" was saluted for an almost perfect landing
"Breaking Bad" was "Mad Men's" former AMC stablemate
The last season of "Mad Men" (first half of season 7) ended with the moon landing in July 1969

stories.story: The last season of "Mad Men," technically the first half of season 7, ended with the moon landing in July 1969. "Mad Men" creator Matthew Weiner and his cast have been typically tight-lipped, with Matthew Weiner even hiding the finale from his cast at first. It is reasonable to assume the new season will pick up soon afterward, as the 1960s scream towards their conclusion, and "Mad Men" probably won't jump ahead much. The latter half of 1969 included the Manson murders, the Woodstock festival, a New York mayoral campaign, and Vietnam War moratorium demonstrations, providing plenty of fodder for the characters.
key_facts: The last season of "Mad Men" (first half of season 7) ended with the moon landing in July 1969
Matthew Weiner, "Mad Men" creator, and his cast are tight-lipped
Matthew Weiner hid the finale from his cast
The new season is expected to pick up soon after July 1969
"Mad Men" probably won't jump ahead much in time
Latter half of 1969 events: Manson murders, Woodstock festival, New York mayoral campaign, Vietnam War moratorium demonstrations
These events provide fodder for "Mad Men" characters

stories.story: The latter half of 1969 included the Manson murders, the Woodstock festival, a New York mayoral campaign, and Vietnam War moratorium demonstrations, offering plenty of fodder for the characters of "Mad Men" to interact with. The show might even mention the Miracle Mets, which would be a nice way to acknowledge the agency's late Lane Pryce. However, "Mad Men" creator Matthew Weiner, who is from Baltimore, might have a different idea for the new season.
key_facts: Latter half of 1969 events: Manson murders, Woodstock festival, New York mayoral campaign, Vietnam War moratorium demonstrations
These events are potential interactions for "Mad Men" characters
The show might mention the Miracle Mets
Mentioning the Miracle Mets would acknowledge the agency's late Lane Pryce
Matthew Weiner, "Mad Men" creator, is from Baltimore and might have different ideas
explicit_unknowns: Whether the show will mention the Miracle Mets
What Matthew Weiner's different idea might be

stories.story: "Mad Men" is generally a show about disintegration, reflective of the 1960s themselves. The old orders are falling apart, with white-shoe WASP firms like Sterling Cooper giving way to ethnic pace-setters such as Doyle Dane Bernbach. Grimy New York is being replaced by sunny Los Angeles, and the "Good War" generation is butting heads with the "Make Love, Not War" cohort. Vacuum tubes and ledger books are being displaced by a sleek, solid-state IBM world, showing the challenges an ad agency faces to keep up.
key_facts: "Mad Men" is about disintegration, reflecting the 1960s
Old orders are falling apart
Sterling Cooper (WASP firm) is giving way to Doyle Dane Bernbach (ethnic pace-setter)
New York is being replaced by Los Angeles
The "Good War" generation is clashing with the "Make Love, Not War" cohort
Old technology (vacuum tubes, ledger books) is being displaced by an IBM world
Ad agencies struggle to keep up with changes

stories.story: Last season of "Mad Men" saw plenty of intraoffice turmoil, thanks to the ill-fitting merger between Sterling Cooper and former rival Cutler Gleason and Chaough. Though the agency survived, it is now without Bert Cooper, played by Robert Morse, and under the ownership of the real-life Madison Avenue titan McCann Erickson. This situation is not a recipe for long-term survival for the agency. Expect a number of longtime characters, including Ken Cosgrove, played by Aaron Staton, Harry Crane, played by Rich Sommer, and perhaps even Joan Harris, played by Christina Hendricks, to look for an exit.
key_facts: Last season of "Mad Men" had intraoffice turmoil
Turmoil was due to the merger between Sterling Cooper and Cutler Gleason and Chaough
The agency survived the merger
The agency is now without Bert Cooper, played by Robert Morse
The agency is owned by McCann Erickson, a real-life Madison Avenue titan
This ownership is not a recipe for long-term survival
Longtime characters Ken Cosgrove (played by Aaron Staton), Harry Crane (played by Rich Sommer), and Joan Harris (played by Christina Hendricks) are expected to look for an exit
explicit_unknowns: Whether Ken Cosgrove, Harry Crane, or Joan Harris will actually exit the agency

stories.story: Roger Sterling, the wisecracking executive played by John Slattery, might find an exit from the agency, but not one he's anticipating. Roger Sterling has suffered two heart attacks and drinks to excess. He has also never grown up, suggesting a volatile future. Bet on a sudden and shocking departure for Roger Sterling.
key_facts: Roger Sterling, the wisecracking executive played by John Slattery, might find an unanticipated exit
Roger Sterling has suffered two heart attacks
Roger Sterling drinks to excess
Roger Sterling has never grown up
A sudden and shocking departure is predicted for Roger Sterling
explicit_unknowns: The exact nature and timing of Roger Sterling's departure

stories.story: Peggy Olson's star has continued to rise, much like advertising wunderkind Mary Wells Lawrence, one of the character's models. Peggy Olson left Sterling Cooper once, and she only returned because her new agency merged with her old one. If Peggy Olson, played by Elisabeth Moss, bolts the firm again, it will probably be to head her own agency. She might also possibly get married, if she is still interested in such an old-fashioned tradition.
key_facts: Peggy Olson's star is rising
Mary Wells Lawrence is an advertising wunderkind and a model for Peggy Olson's character
Peggy Olson left Sterling Cooper previously
Peggy Olson returned to Sterling Cooper due to a merger
If Peggy Olson, played by Elisabeth Moss, leaves the firm, it will likely be to head her own agency
Peggy Olson might possibly get married
Her interest in marriage depends on her view of it as an old-fashioned tradition
explicit_unknowns: Whether Peggy Olson will bolt the firm
Whether Peggy Olson will head her own agency
Whether Peggy Olson will get married

stories.thread: Character Fates - Betty and Sally Draper
stories.story: In recent seasons, Don's ex-wife, Betty, played by January Jones, has lost herself amid all the turmoil. Betty sees herself through the eyes of her spouses. Though her husband Henry Francis, played by Christopher Stanley, has been far more supportive than Don, Henry Francis is a busy man.
key_facts: Betty, Don's ex-wife, played by January Jones, has lost herself in recent seasons
Betty sees herself through her spouses' eyes
Henry Francis, Betty's husband, played by Christopher Stanley, is more supportive than Don
Henry Francis is a busy man

stories.thread: Character Fates - Betty and Sally Draper
stories.story: Sally, Don's daughter, played by Kiernan Shipka, is proving to be a handful. She is obviously bright but, as a teenager, is starting to rebel. In recent seasons, Sally has run away and started sneaking cigarettes, and she is always fighting with her mother, Betty. One could imagine Sally hitchhiking to Woodstock or at least dropping out of school.
key_facts: Sally, Don's daughter, played by Kiernan Shipka, is a handful
Sally is bright
Sally is a rebellious teenager
Sally has run away and started sneaking cigarettes in recent seasons
Sally is always fighting with her mother, Betty
Potential actions for Sally: hitchhiking to Woodstock, dropping out of school
explicit_unknowns: Whether Sally will hitchhike to Woodstock or drop out of school

stories.story: Don Draper has been through divorces, affairs, office politics, and morose late-night rides with Glen Bishop. A predicted scene for Don Draper is set on April 1, 1970, when Richard Nixon is signing legislation banning cigarette ads on radio and television. This event reminds Don Draper of the day 10 years earlier when he came up with the Lucky Strike campaign that began the series. No fool, Don Draper had seen this day coming years before.
key_facts: Don Draper's past includes divorces, affairs, office politics, and late-night rides with Glen Bishop
A predicted scene for Don Draper is on April 1, 1970
On April 1, 1970, Richard Nixon is signing legislation banning cigarette ads on radio and television
This event reminds Don Draper of the Lucky Strike campaign 10 years earlier, which began the series
Don Draper had foreseen the cigarette ad ban years before
explicit_unknowns: Whether this predicted scene will actually happen

stories.story: On April 1, 1970, after Richard Nixon signs legislation banning cigarette ads on radio and television, Don Draper will fix himself a drink. He will ponder buying an avocado-colored refrigerator and clean out his ashtray. Don Draper is predicted to leave the show the way he arrived: on top of the zeitgeist, unable to accept his past, and utterly, inscrutably alone.
key_facts: Predicted actions for Don Draper on April 1, 1970: fix a drink, ponder buying an avocado-colored refrigerator, clean ashtray
Don Draper is predicted to leave the show on top of the zeitgeist
Don Draper is predicted to leave the show unable to accept his past
Don Draper is predicted to leave the show utterly, inscrutably alone
explicit_unknowns: Whether this predicted final act for Don Draper will actually happen
